skill.name: creating-issues
skill.description: Creates well-structured GitHub issues with title conventions, labels, scope detection, templates, milestones, and project board guidance.
skill.summary: Create consistent GitHub issues with validated titles, labels, scopes, and structured bodies.
skill.author: C0ntr0lledCha0s

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many weak-cryptography and shell-execution patterns, but the weak-cryptography alerts are false positives from issue text, markdown examples, or validation wording. Confirmed concerns are legitimate operational risks: Python helpers and skill guidance can run GitHub CLI commands, read local project context, query GitHub metadata, and create or modify issues and project board entries.

medium_findings.title: GitHub CLI issue creation can modify repositories
medium_findings.description: The helper builds a gh issue create command from user-provided issue metadata and executes it with subprocess.run. Arguments are passed as a list, so shell injection is not evident, but the command can create issues and assign labels, milestones, projects, and assignees in the active repository.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: Direct subprocess execution of gh issue create is present, and the command performs repository mutation. Confidence is not higher because arguments are not passed through a shell, reducing injection risk.

medium_findings.title: GitHub CLI API calls expose repository and project metadata
medium_findings.description: The helper queries repository milestones and viewer projects through gh api and GraphQL. This is expected for issue management, but it can transmit authenticated repository context to GitHub and print project information.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The API calls are explicit and use authenticated GitHub CLI behavior. The intent appears legitimate, so the issue is operational exposure rather than malicious exfiltration.

medium_findings.title: Skill guidance encourages direct project board mutations
medium_findings.description: The skill instructs the agent to inspect project board context and run gh project item-add. This matches the stated workflow, but it can modify project boards if used without user confirmation.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The instructions explicitly include the project board command and mandate adding issues when project context exists. The risk depends on user authorization and GitHub CLI permissions.

low_findings.title: Local project context is read for scope detection
low_findings.description: The scope detector reads .claude/github-workflows/env.json, inspects top-level directories, and reads git-conventional-commits.json when present. This is relevant to the feature, but it should be disclosed because it reads local project metadata.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The filesystem reads are clear and limited to project metadata. No evidence found of secret harvesting or broad recursive file collection.

low_findings.title: Bulk label migration command can mass-edit issues if copied
low_findings.description: A markdown reference shows a pipeline that lists issues, pipes numbers through jq and xargs, and edits labels in bulk. It is documentation rather than hidden execution, but copying it can modify many GitHub issues.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The command is visible documentation, so it is likely intentional. It still has real side effects if a user or agent runs it without narrowing the issue set.

low_findings.title: Weak cryptography alerts are false positives
low_findings.description: The flagged template and guide lines contain normal issue text, examples, or words such as hash in environment fields. No evidence found of cryptographic implementation or use of weak cryptographic algorithms.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Targeted review of the flagged template lines showed markdown placeholders, not cryptographic code. Similar static hits in references are caused by issue-writing examples and text formatting.

dangerous_patterns.title: subprocess.run executes GitHub CLI commands
dangerous_patterns.description: The helper centralizes GitHub CLI execution through subprocess.run with a gh argument vector. This avoids shell expansion but still enables authenticated repository operations.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The subprocess call is direct and the command target is GitHub CLI. Risk is bounded by argument-list execution and authenticated CLI permissions.

dangerous_patterns.title: GitHub issue creation command is assembled from user input
dangerous_patterns.description: The create_issue function accepts title, body, scope, branch, milestone, project, and assignee values, then adds them to gh issue create arguments. This can create public or private repository content containing user-provided text.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The data flow from parsed arguments to gh issue create is clear. No shell injection is evident, but repository mutation and data publication are confirmed.

dangerous_patterns.title: Documentation includes bulk xargs issue-edit command
dangerous_patterns.description: The label migration guide includes an xargs pipeline that can apply a label to every matching issue. It is not auto-executed, but it is a high-impact example command.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The static xargs alert is real as a documented command, but there is no evidence that the skill executes it automatically.

content.value_statement: Poorly formed issues slow triage and create missing metadata. This skill helps Claude, Codex, and Claude Code draft issues with clear titles, labels, scopes, templates, and project context.
actual_capabilities: Validates issue titles for prefixes, length, vague wording, and imperative style.
Suggests required type, priority, and scope labels for GitHub issues.
Drafts standard, bug, and feature issue bodies with acceptance criteria.
Detects scope from environment data, branch names, and configured project scopes.
Lists labels, milestones, and projects through the GitHub CLI when available.
Provides commands and guidance for project board placement and issue relationships.
limitations: GitHub issue creation requires an authenticated GitHub CLI in the target repository.
Ambiguous priority, scope, milestone, or relationship choices still require user confirmation.
Parent-child issue relationships depend on separate GraphQL tooling or another skill.
Scope detection is limited to configured environment data, branch names, and simple project structure checks.

use_cases.title: Prepare a Bug Report
use_cases.description: Turn a bug description into a clear title, required labels, reproduction steps, and acceptance criteria.

use_cases.title: Plan Feature Work
use_cases.description: Create feature issues with use cases, proposed solution notes, scope labels, and milestone suggestions.

use_cases.title: Standardize Repository Triage
use_cases.description: Apply consistent label rules and issue body structure across a GitHub repository.

prompt_templates.title: Draft a Basic Issue
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner request for one issue draft.
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a GitHub issue for fixing login timeout errors. Suggest a title, labels, and an issue body.

prompt_templates.title: Validate an Issue Title
prompt_templates.scenario: Check whether a proposed title follows conventions.
prompt_templates.prompt: Review this issue title and suggest a better version if needed: [BUG] Search breaks on empty input.

prompt_templates.title: Create Metadata for a Feature
prompt_templates.scenario: Build a complete issue package with labels and scope.
prompt_templates.prompt: Write a feature issue for adding CSV export to the reports page. Include type, priority, scope, acceptance criteria, and out-of-scope notes.

prompt_templates.title: Plan a Linked Issue Set
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced planning for related work across projects and milestones.
prompt_templates.prompt: Break the authentication redesign into linked GitHub issues. Ask for missing project board, milestone, parent-child, priority, and scope details before creating anything.

output_examples.input: Create an issue for the settings page losing unsaved changes.
output: Suggested title: Fix unsaved settings changes during navigation
Body includes summary, reproduction steps, expected behavior, actual behavior, acceptance criteria, and environment notes.

output_examples.input: Is this title good: [FEATURE] Add dark mode?
output: The title should not include a type prefix because the type belongs in a label.
Suggested title: Add dark mode support

output_examples.input: Create issues for a reporting dashboard launch.
output: The skill asks whether to use a project board, milestone, and parent-child structure first.
It then proposes a parent issue, implementation issues, documentation tasks, labels, and acceptance criteria.

best_practices: Confirm scope, priority, milestone, and project board before creating issues.
Use labels for type and priority instead of adding prefixes to titles.
Write acceptance criteria that are specific, measurable, and testable.
anti_patterns: Creating issues without a scope label or clear owner.
Using vague titles such as Fix bug, Update code, or Add feature.
Creating project board or relationship changes without user confirmation.

faq.question: Can this skill create GitHub issues automatically?
faq.answer: Yes, it includes helper commands that use the GitHub CLI when the user approves and the CLI is authenticated.

faq.question: Does it require GitHub CLI?
faq.answer: Only direct repository actions require GitHub CLI. Drafting titles, labels, and issue bodies does not require it.

faq.question: What labels does it expect?
faq.answer: It expects one type label, one priority label, and at least one scope label for every issue.

faq.question: Can it handle bug and feature templates?
faq.answer: Yes. The skill includes standard issue, bug report, and feature request templates.

faq.question: Can it create parent-child issue relationships?
faq.answer: It can identify relationship opportunities, but true parent-child links require separate GraphQL relationship tooling.

faq.question: Is this skill safe for public marketplace use?
faq.answer: It is publishable with warnings because it can run GitHub CLI commands and read local project context.
